answer:
The work is *Old English Elegies*, chapter 13 ("Welund him be wurman wraeces cunnade"), commonly known as the Old English poem *Deor*. This chapter features a stanzaic catalogue of legendary misfortunes—Weland bound by Niðhad, Beadohild's pregnancy, Mæðhild and Geat's excessive love, Theodric's long exile among the Mærings, and Eormanric's wolfish rule over the Goths—each closing with the refrain "That passed over; so may this." The speaker, a displaced *scop* (poet) of the Heodenings, identifies himself as Deor, supplanted by the rival Heorrenda.[^1]

Three occurrences of the refrain (from the stanzas on Weland, Eormanric, and Deor himself) are as follows:

> Weland knew well the worm’s cruel bite, [...] supple sinew-bonds on a better man. **That passed over; so may this.**[^2]

> We have heard tell of Eormanric's wolfish mind; [...] that the kingdom itself might be overcome. **That passed over; so may this.**[^3]

> This I can say about my own self, [...] which the protector of earls had granted me before. **That passed over; so may this.**[^4]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 13, [para.](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-6b7e51).
[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 13, [para.](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-1308a0).
[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 13, [para.](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-160f1c).
[^4]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 13, [para.](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-6b7e51).
